A variety of antiretroviral agents are recognized for their ability to prevent acquisition of HIV, including the non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) delavirdine, delavirdine‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine, and doravirine, as well as the integrase inhibitors dolutegravir, raltegravir, and cabotegravir. Among these, cabotegravir is also noted for its therapeutic role in treating established HIV infection. Delavirdine, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine, and doravirine are all members of the class “Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors,” while dolutegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir belong to the class “Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use.” Doravirine carries a specific contraindication with the antimicrobial rifapentine, indicating that co‑administration should be avoided. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how both NNRTIs and integrase inhibitors contribute to HIV prevention strategies—such as pre‑exposure prophylaxis—and, in the case of cabotegravir, to ongoing treatment regimens, underscoring the importance of class‑based drug selection and awareness of drug‑specific contraindications in comprehensive patient care.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2310) Fosamprenavir, indinavir, and tipranavir are all members of the protease inhibitor class, which constitutes a group of direct‑acting antiviral agents. As protease inhibitors, each drug targets viral protease enzymes, thereby blocking the proteolytic processing of viral polyproteins that is essential for viral replication. This mechanism makes them key components of antiretroviral therapy, particularly in the management of HIV infection, where inhibition of the HIV protease enzyme reduces viral load and slows disease progression. Recognizing that fosamprenavir, indinavir, and tipranavir share this common pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimen components and understand their collective role in disrupting viral life cycles.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156) Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the class of integrase inhibitors that are used as systemic anti‑infective agents. As integrase inhibitors, these drugs target the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby forming a key component of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection. Recognizing that each of these agents belongs to the same pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, understand class‑related efficacy and safety profiles, and manage drug interactions in the care of patients living with HIV.

Integrase inhibitors (cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir) block the viral integrase enzyme required for proviral integration; NNRTIs (delavirdine, rilpivirine, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine) bind a distinct site on reverse transcriptase, with rilpivirine contraindicated when co‑administered with the proton‑pump inhibitor pantoprazole. Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 0 -- Community 3) of HIV is linked to shared needle use. Treatment agents include cabotegravir and fostemsavir. NRTIs such as tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine and zidovudine act as nucleoside analogues that terminate viral DNA synthesis during reverse transcription. Integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir—block integration of viral DNA into the host genome. NNRTIs—including doravirine, delavirdine, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapin and rilpivirine—bind a distinct site on reverse transcriptase. Recognized drug‑interaction contraindications are doravirine with the rifamycin rifapentine and pantoprazole with rilpivirine, underscoring the importance of class‑specific knowledge for safe patient management. In addition to DAAs, a distinct group of nucleosides and nucleotides that are not reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors comprises brincidofovir, brivudine and remdesivir. Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1571) Lovastatin, a cholesterol‑lowering statin, is contraindicated for coadministration with a range of antiretroviral agents used in the treatment of HIV and related viral infections. Specifically, the protease inhibitors lopinavir, atazanavir sulfate, darunavir, fosamprenavir calcium, saquinavir mesylate, and the NS3/4A protease inhibitor paritaprevir should not be taken together with lovastatin. In addition, the non‑nucleoside reverse‑transcriptase inhibitor dasabuvir sodium monohydrate and the pharmacokinetic enhancer kobicistat are also listed as contraindicated with lovastatin. These contraindications reflect the potential for significant drug‑drug interactions, often mediated by inhibition of the CYP3A4 enzyme, which can lead to elevated lovastatin concentrations and an increased risk of serious adverse effects such as myopathy or rhabdomyolysis. For safe patient care, clinicians must avoid prescribing lovastatin alongside any of these antiretroviral medications and consider alternative lipid‑lowering therapies when treating patients receiving HIV‑directed regimens.
